item.title: I thought oracles were tough to make/maintain? — 22 replies — 88.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
I thought oracles were tough to make/maintain?

Open thread • Replies: 22 • Updated: 2026-03-16 04:03 UTC

OP

I thought oracles were tough to make/maintain? Why didn't Hyperliquid bend the knee to Chainlink? If the most used crypto project doesn't use Chainlink, why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?

Early replies (first 3)

61985818
>>61985727
>why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?
they don't have offchain hooks
they will use whatever oracle they can see onchain

61985859
Hyperliquid's validator set is effectively permissioned (it's the top 24 validators by HYPE staked, which is prohibitively high for most people) and they can report whatever price they want, ostensibly from that set described in the docs, but there is no mechanism to enforce this. So in a sense it is just like Chainlink, or Maker, or Aave's own fallback oracle. Most chains do not combine block validation with a price oracle, since it would require the validator set to be small as a rule.

61986446
>>61985727
This is becoming a lot more common. Or native oracles. Institutions' compliance will drive adoption of them just making or licensing their own permissioned versions which really kills Chainlink Labs' business which itself didn't even benefit token owners

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61986455
>>61985727
HYPE just hasn't suffered 10 figures of loss from an oracle failure yet. It'll happen eventually.

61986468
because fatoshi is stuck in defi era and probably doesnt know what hyperliquid is

61986471
you can deploy your own oracle with claude for free

61986792
>>61986455
>It'll happen eventually
yup. history has shown that a great liquidity concentrated in a place with a weak oracle is bound to be messed with and the money gone eventually. there is no two ways about it kek

61987083
>>61985727
hes cookin

61987131
owning link instead of hype is a sign of brain damage

61987174
>>61986792
You don't even know how their oracles work.

They rely on 24 validators that take a weighted average from different platforms. Chainlink literally does this for each price feed.

The only manipulation is where illiquid shitcoins listed on the exchange can be pumped and dumped.

So no, you're entire Chainlink oracle domination thesis, and "everything gets hacked" is nonsense.

61987175
>>61987157
>look mom I posted it again!

61987270
>>61986455
>>61987083
It worked flawlessly on 10/10.

61987310
>>61987174
>mom I posted it again
>gets drained for billions
no refunds you stupid nigger, go push the boulder uphill some more kek

61987499
>>61985727
>f the most used crypto project doesn't use Chainlink, why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?
Hyperliquid has two major components: HyperCore and HyperEVM.

Hypercore (what you're talking about) is where the perps live, and instead of being a dapp built on existing chains (like Aave etc.) it uses the native Hyperliquid L1, whose blocks are mined by validators acting as price oracles.
Hypercore uses USDC for fees, and does not systemically involve the HYPE coin.
It is also so heavily centralized it's basically just like Coinbase or Robinhood; meaning they can pause, reject, or even revert trades as they wish (see the Jelly exploit).

Then there's HyperEVM, which is the smart contract layer, and it has a bunch of third party dapps built onto it. The HYPE coin is used here for gas. These dapps tend to use Chainlink.

61987524
The native perps on HL use their own oracle yes.
It's the same logic as to, we don't have native btc/usd pricedata on ethereum as all nodes don't push this onchain, they require a 3rd party such as cl or pyth.
However, for custom deployed perps, these will use 3rd party oracles. Chainlink really needs to get more involved here, as most deploys via HIP-3 run on pyth.

61988746
>>61985727
Guess who was right?

61988795
>>61988746
As a PNK baggie, fuck you

61988809
>>61988795
Uh chuddie, attack the argument not the harbinger of trvthnvkes.

61988980
>>61988746
Where to buy?

61989211
>>61988980
Unilever sir.

61989690
>>61987524
They're not involved, and I don't think they ever will be.

If hyperliquid ever get big enough then their price feeds will be worth more than Chainlinks. They could even pivot to Oracles and take some of their business.

People don't even understand what's going on. I don't even hold Hype btw, I'm actually a CL baggie.

Open thread • Replies: 20 • Updated: 2026-03-16 04:02 UTC

OP

I've got 50k in a savings account and about 15k in the market (VOO, etc), do I keep DCAing? What if the market crashes?

Early replies (first 3)

61987565
I’d keep it exactly as is for now. We should get a much bigger pullback in stocks this year from the unfolding liquidity crisis.

61987573
>>61987552
Buy a few oz of silver just in case.

61987574
>>61987552
Do you have anything of value to offer to the world? If so, invest in that.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61987576
>>61987552
Wait a few more weeks for the big economic downturn and then invest it all in a Global Index fund.

61987604
>>61987552
>do I keep DCAing?
yes
>What if the market crashes
a market crash is also called "a sale". You keep DCAing. Unless you prefer to buy high instead of buying low, in which case knock yourself out.

61987619

61987723
>>61987552
Wait for the turbo crash and put at least $1k into XRP and hold it like a savings account for a long time.
>>61987619
This is definitely not the worst thing he can do if markets are set to crash in unison. Lol Stop paying and listening to a financial advisor who is paid a fee to buy you index funds and still underperforms the market.

61987727
>>61987552
>50k in a savings account
ok so you invest it and either
>nothing happens and you make a modest 7-15%
>something happens and market collapses and fed actions cause sharp inflation and you lose 7-8% anyway

61987738
>>61987565
people said this at the covid low. op do as you feel.

61987741
>>61987552
Uphold (the crypto exchange) has a partnership checking account that is FDIC insured up to 2.25 million with deposits swept across 12 megabanks. It pays 3.75% APY paid out monthly which for you is almost $2k in annual interest just for holding with free ability to buy move to exchanges or brokerages to buy and sell and spend etc. with no lockups like a savings account that likely pays less. Most banks are paying literally .01% on deposits and are more exposed to systemic banking risk. Additionally, Uphold offers 2 or 4% (depending on what card you choose, the 2% is free but the 4% costs $100 per year) in XRP paid back on what you spend from your USD account, which is really nicely segmented from your interest account so you only want to keep in your other USD account what you intend to spend imminently and you can move between them in literally 10 seconds at a moment's notice. If you choose to use the account to deposit your paycheck you get another 4% in XRP, which is literally just like getting a 4% raise instantly and a 2-4% cut to expenses (other than credit card bills). So you get 3.75% on deposits held, 4% on deposited paycheck and 2-4% on what you spend. So far the most competitive way to hold cash I have seen. You could buy STRC and earn the divi payment as a cash position but if everything crashes crypto will also and you are massively risking your money by doing so. Hold strong cash and buy the real bottom.

We haven't even begun to see the real fallout of the private credit crash or the backlog energy experience from the oil situation etc. You are literally comparing the beginning of inklings of drop to the time the largest fiscal stimulus package (with zero reserve requirement enacted) in US history. Lol

OP

I just spent 3 days working/chilling with people worth over 100 mil in a major city. I am a poor.

AMA

Early replies (first 3)

61984154
>>61984144

61984159

Unfortunately my friend’s whore wife’s cousin got caught funneling money from his business and the whore sided with the cousin and ended up divorcing my friend, haven’t really see him since then

61984200
>>61984154
These ones didn't care about mine. They just say "oh cool" and move on, that was last time I chilled with them. We bonded with one because he gets employees from Eastern Europe and underpays them and I am from Eastern Europe, so I made jokes about how he has slave labor, he enjoyed that one. I grew up in a room in a communal Soviet block with a shared bathroom with no shower, so I have good ones, like the neighbor prostitutes autistic son drawing on the walls with shit while she is whoring in the room next door. Her titties were fine though, sometimes I wonder if she would have let me touch them at 7 years old, because why not, whore anyway.

>>61984159
I am not that poor biw, I am just old and a wagie, my poor friends have new Supras and 700whp MR2s with smooth boost that keep up with ZR1s, so M3 comp wouldn't be an event for me.

The rich fags absolutely do not flex cars since it's considered tacky. A previous story from chilling with them is that one of the kids at the company cashed in his equity and got a 718 and then the founder brutally smoked him in his Plaid. Note - I am not a fan of Tesla, but it was still funny. I think the kid didn't really know how to drop gears to drag either, so it was just bad.

But yea, these fags flex their private jet (emphasis on jet, not plane), but they still share it.

Oh, and they made fun of Cincinnati. They went to Jeff Ruby's and one of them cut up the table cloth and ran around with it.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61984482
>>61984144
I get the impression people like that are rather insulated and uninteresting in anything not related to their exact field of work. As in having the most normie (or at best pseud) views on politics, philosophy, history, etc. I assume thats why people like crowly and rasputin where able to walk circles around the upperclass in spite being insane
Does that line up with your experience?

61984538
>>61984482
It's a little bit more nuanced than that.

>I get the impression people like that are rather insulated and uninteresting in anything not related to their exact field of work
This is largely correct. They are extremely focused on their work and spend most of their day working on their business. Note that "business" isn't some vague word that MBA kids think it is. If they work on servers, they spend all day working on servers. If they work on small satellites, they spend all day on it. That does includes sales, conferences, etc.

The one difference I found between them and me (and other loser nerds) is that it's not conceptual either - they don't spend time chasing vague theory, like "but what if blah", they always ask what's in the market no, what's realistic, and is it actually going to give an advantage over competitors and/or for sales.

They are also obsessed over money and flexing on their other rich friends, but again, literally just with the number, not any material things.

>As in having the most normie (or at best pseud) views on politics, philosophy, history, etc.
They don't really do the theorizing part that we do on here, but that's not to say they can't think critically / consider the other possibilities. Let's say you say that 9/11 was a false flag. They will just "sure, that makes sense, governments have done insider t attacks to drum up support for military actions". If you go more into the /pol/ side of things about the owner, how he wasn't there, what country he is associated with, etc, they will also not dismiss it, just say it makes sense. But they are not going to dwell on it or speak about it for days.

They also have conversations like "The US is more communist than X European country, because in X country we couldn't get funding to do research, but the US gave us a bil so they don't fall behind globally."
(cont.)

61984541
tldr: Not dumb, but definitely not dwelling on shit like "are aliens real" - they understand that it doesn't matter to them day-to-day.

>>61984482
> I assume thats why people like crowly and rasputin where able to walk circles around the upperclass in spite being insane
Now this is the key point which is wrong - both Crowley and Rasputin ran circles around idiots. There is a clear distinction between idiotic old money (and not all of them are) and people who do shit and can differentiate bs from fiction.

I am going to pretend I am rich and go high level - Rasputin ran circles around spoiled court-ladies and the empress who didn't do shit, were bored, open to mysticism and didn't get good dick. They WANTED to believe him and had nothing on the line (well, except the whole imperial family eventually losing influence and being assassinated), but you get the gist.

Crowley never achieved shit after he became an occultist - he rode on his old money and reputation as a chess player and alpinist, but these guys would absolutely NOT believe him, because the immediate question is "what practical problems do you solve?" and the answer is "nothing", and that ends the convo.

P.S. Comte of St.Germain would probably be a better example, but they wouldn't believe him either.

Short answer - no, and not just for occultists, any other bsers.

Also, people always say "network with rich people" - the reality is that only works if you have real alignment, like they sell serves and you sell RAM, then they MIGHT go with you over a competitor if you can talk through a decent deal and they know they can knock on your door to solve problems. But there is never any magic "networking" effect.

Hard no to your original question, unless they are the true generate multi-generation wealth which is actually rare, because they still have to appear competitive to peers.

61984557
>>61984482
And finally, there is a good chance they don't really know who Crowley and St.Germain are and haven't heard of them, because they didn't spend time reading occult shit to begin with.

They will know of Rasputin exactly as a history book describes them, but you and I spend way too much time reading useless shit. In that way, we "fell for it" more than they did, since we are researching something that essentially has no value just so we can mumble obscure shit. I will say that because of looking into, we are probably more resilient to cults, silly ideas, etc. However, the rich fags I am talking about just dismiss them outright. They are literally working on their business or going out with models (or their wives in many cases) while you and I shitpost here. And it adds up. I work really hard, but I still shitpost in between, they don't. They really do "work hard/party hard". I thought I worked hard too, but the fact that my top entertainment is posting here does kill motivation and I drag work out because I haven't hard real success. They stay motivated.

61984690
>>61984557
> I work really hard, but I still shitpost in between, they don't.

they've got that grindset mindset which is like the left end of the IQ bell curve really, isn't it? they end up being about as interesting to talk to as a delivery driver, at the end of the day.

And they never ever stop watching every word that comes out of their mouths. There's never a slip of the tongue. Just like low IQ retards are sweating over 'err did i just say the wrong thing?? no u said the wrong thing! no wait did i say it', so are rich people.

61985012
>>61984538
>>61984541
>>61984557
Interesting, thanks for the insights anon!

61987443
>>61984538
Seems like an awful way to live man.

61987540
>>61987443
Well of course, they're still dumb normalfags even if they're rich. None of that shit applies to rich autists who have made it through advanced nomismancy.

61987543
Thanks for the info OP. Always nice to learn more about those kinds of people.
Were there a lot of jews?

61987556
>>61984144
Did the richfags seem happy? I have this friend, he's among the top 10 richfags (old, old money) in our Town of 50.000, and he's always melancholic, overworked, lonely and it gets worse when he's drunk. You'd think Richfags have to be happier on average

61989087
>>61984144
they like having you around in the same way a pretty girl likes having an ugly friend.

61989355
>>61984538

61989483
>>61984144
How does it feel to be a gay prostitute and think the depraved self-extinction you experience is luxury?

61989494
>>61984144
Cool, who cares?
Like, are they giving you money to stop working?

That'd be the only reason I'd hang with them.

61989536
There are all kinds of different rich people. Conspiratard fellow travelers sometimes do get rich and they don't stop being conspiratards. If they're the business type they probably turn it off for longer periods, but my conspiratarded rich friends are VERY likely to sit up all night chatting with me about aliens, archons, what have you.

I have also known think tank idea men who got rich in that field and they are constantly full of nutty ideas. But also had extensive mental rulesets to evaluate these ideas.

I think the one constant thing is, whatever they are, it is taken to a depth.

61989597
I have a good friend who has the the net worth of a couple million dollars with a caveat. He's in the shithole third world country I'm from - so that sort of money means he is rich.

We both grew up at the same place, went to the same school.

What others have said is very accurate. There was a great difference to me and him as kids. I was trying to understand how to navigate the world around me, I would read the Greeks, Tolkien, as many stories and books as I could to simply try to understand the truth and how things worked. It was like I was learning everything from step zero.

My friend on the other hand, from as early as 10-11 already understood very well social status, types of employment, what each sort of professional made. He was very observational - he'd look at someone and say "that lady is married with the guy that works at this place, makes this much, but his car is not a Jetta, his kid is lying about that", essentially, he was very materialistic, innately clever, and shrewd, while I was more interested in the natural world, truth, etc.

We bonded because we were both nerdy kids, not too popular, liked the same videogames, and were similar in our disdain for the "mass".

Over the years I have to say a lot of his cleverness and pragmatism rubbed off on me. After all the searching, all roads lead to Rome - what matters in reality is making money and gaining power.

I've learned that since he is not a naturally inquisitive person, and he doesn't view the world as "true" or "not true", he won't really engage in topics for the sake of discussion, like White people do. So when speaking to him I'll drop a nugget of information or a meme, and if he finds it funny, or asks for more information I'll then send more info.

61989668
>>61985012
Welcome

>>61987543
> Were there a lot of jews?
None out of the rich people I mentioned, but I am sure it's also just depends on the group. All of these were from a European country, think Denmark or Norway or something. City in question isn't in Europe though, but they all moved there and started businesses.

Went to an investment event for hyper rich fags, most of the financial analysts there were Jewish. Did not impress me at all, just talked about how put options hedge positions like it's an advanced strategy. I was pretty disappointed, it's the type of shit you learn in finance 101 or Series 7/63. No mind-blowing info, zero.

item.content:encoded:
Ripple cucking XRP holders again

Open thread • Replies: 60 • Updated: 2026-03-16 02:35 UTC

OP

Early replies (first 3)

61970723
>>61970713
>xerp
>creep toes
pick one!

61971052
>>61970713
89383 xrp left

61971076
>>61970713

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61974410
>>61974383
>Eth literally had an ICO
And? As long as they didn't promise to make the price pump (like Ripple did) there's nothing wrong with that.

>>61974399
>The waiver is literally pronouncement of this.
The waiver grants Ripple use of Regulation D exemptions. No other crypto needs Regulation D exemptions to sell to institutions.

61974481
>>61974410
Lol And yet others who were were blocked and XRP fit Hinman's speech (which the SEC to tje judge's scorn withheld related discovery of well past the deadline) criteria much more than ETH for safe passage, already had clarity at FinCen (the US Treasury) and Ripple had consulted extensively with the SEC prior to the suit. See full timeline:
https://x.com/JohnEDeaton1/status/1494924825407668224

WAIVER CONDITION LITERALLY DOESN'T MATTER OR DISTINGUISH SALES. You are basically arguing that dad said Timmy couldn't go to the sleepover so he asked his mom and mom has veto power to the dad's determination so Timmy ended up being able to go. But you are Timmy's friend sitting there at the sleep over saying "nuh uh! Timmy can't REALLY go to the sleepover, his dad originally said he couldn't so he isn't really there like all the other kids! Such a low IQ argument it is actually literally unbelievable you think you are doing anything meaningful saying it. If it mattered at all Ripple would have not have dropped their appeal and they did drop it because it is functionally indistinguishable.

61974541
>>61974481
>WAIVER CONDITION LITERALLY DOESN'T MATTER OR DISTINGUISH SALES

The injunction only applied to direct institutional sales. The waiver means Ripple can use Regulation D exemptions from the injunction, implying a bunch of rules and restrictions that no other crypto has to abide by.
I'm done repeating myself.

61974584
>>61974541
Again, they literally can sell explicitly to the same people in exactly the same form of offering. You are coping INSANELY. Revisit the mummy sleepover veto analogy. Lol

61974603
>>61974584
Show us one other crypto that has to use Regulation D exemptions to sell directly to (institutional) buyers.

/discussion

61974686
>>61974603
Again, it is literally irrelevant and means absolutely nothing to sales or Ripple or institutions which buy. Lol But still, regulation D (particularly Rule 506(b) or 506(c)) is one of the most common U.S. securities law exemptions used by crypto and blockchain companies to sell their tokens/coins. It allows private placements to accredited investors (with no limit on the amount raised under Rule 506) without full SEC registration, often structured via Simple Agreements for Future Tokens. the bad:
>slow fucking tx speed due to small mempool size: nobody is going to wait in the parking lot for 20 minutes to wait for the tx to confirm after you sell them a ps5. XRP is seconds.
>high tx fees that will get worse: no tail emission, no inflation. so tha tmeans miners need to charger more tx fees to make up for no coins being able to be mined anymore. besides that, it's already high as it is now
>only basic scripting? seriously? no smart contracts? the fuck, what fucking utility?
>traceable: great for governments. but makes your btc non-fungible because what happens if it traces back to a drug cartel LMAO. now you have tainted money, it cannot be treated like cash.

but yeah: high tx fees, slow uncomfortable confirmation and especially lack of scripting make it bad.

however, the one... BEST thing for bitcoin is that it is very good for money laundering. bitcoin has the strongest legal "foothold" in being hard to remove. So, it has use... But I'd like to see why people think bitcoin is #1 besides emotional sentiment. we all still love bitcoin but man, the utility barely checks out.

>>61983560
>recognized as a CURRENCY by US government

No cryptocurrency is recognized as currency. If you pay capital gains tax when you exchange it for goods or services that is by definition not currency. Bitcoin is recognized as a commodity, or property. This will literally never change unless governments one-day decide to act against their own economic interests (they will not do this.)

>slow fucking tx speed due to small mempool size: nobody is going to wait in the parking lot for 20 minutes to wait for the tx to confirm after you sell them a ps5. XRP is seconds.

Here is the big pill for you dumb XRP niggers to swallow. There isn't really a usecase for any crypto "currency" to be actually used as currency. Fiat currency works fine for day-to-day transactions unless there is Zimbabwe level hyperinflation and your paycheck is worthless a day after you receive it. People will continue to use crypto to circumvent online gambling laws or buy drugs, but that is all it has proven to be useful for currency-wise. Maybe blockchain tech will be utilized with stable coins but that is essentially just fiat currency on crypto rails.

The real problem with fiat currency is its inability to allow you to carry value through time. Gold has proven to be the best commodity to store value throughout history due to its scarcity and durability. Bitcoin is the evolution of gold, hence the moniker "digital gold." It solves all the problems of gold and improves upon it. That is the true value.

item.title: the BTC bears are genuinely not impressed by the last move. — 6 replies — 20.6/hr
item.content:encoded:
the BTC bears are genuinely not impressed by the last move.

Open thread • Replies: 6 • Updated: 2026-03-16 03:54 UTC

OP

the BTC bears are genuinely not impressed by the last move.

btc is up 7.8% in 6 days. every day green. and the short wallets I track (a bit like ELO ranking in chess) went from 548 to 568. they added during the rally.

they got in at $74k average. they're sitting on 4% gains right now. a whole week of green candles and their position got worse by 6%. that's it. they're not scared, they have no reason to close.

$74,285 is the number. that's where $1.67 billion in shorts are breakeven all at once. btc pushes through that with volume and the covering could cascade into a real squeeze. btc stalls in the 73-74k zone and starts fading, the bears just survived a 6 day rally and the move is probably over.

the thing that's odd to me is the wallets with the best track records in this dataset cut their longs by 38% during this rally. they're not short either. they just stopped adding.

so you've got a real rally, bears unmoved, and the best longs reducing. $74k tells you which side was right.

tldr: bears holding, watch $74,285.
